topics: 🐂 שור שנגח ארבעה וחמשה שוורים — how the gored owners get paid
⚖️ R' Meir vs. R' Yehudah: did the נזק acquire the goring ox or only get a pledge?
💍 הקדש, הוקיר, שכר, השביח — four practical tests of the dispute
👥 R' Yehudah vs. R' Shimon: 'קנה' for living and dead, or only the dead
💰 שלשה שהטילו לכיס — how partners with unequal investments split gains and losses

overview.title: 📖 Overview — Who Owns the Goring Ox?
overview.label: The sugya:
overview.text: The Mishnah pictures a tam ox that gores several other oxen and is now owed to many victims. R' Yehudah and R' Meir disagree about whether the damaged party becomes a partial owner of the goring ox or only holds it as a pledge. The Yerushalmi runs the dispute through four practical scenarios — dedication, price-rise, rental, and improvement — to show what is at stake. The sugya then shifts to a related partnership Mishnah: when three put unequal amounts into a common purse, how do they share gains and losses?
overview.hebrewSource: שׁוֹר שֶׁנָּגַח אַרְבָּעָה וַחֲמִשָּׁה שְׁוָורִין... שְׁמִירַת נְזָקִין כִּשְׁמִירַת קִנְיָין

slides.title: 💬 R' Meir's Two-Sided Attack
slides.subtitle: R' Yannai sets up R' Meir's dilemma against the goring ox's owner.

lines.speaker: RY
lines.text: R' Yannai: בָּא עָלָיו רִבִּי מֵאִיר מִשְּׁנֵי צְדָדִין — R' Meir comes at him from two sides.

lines.speaker: RM
lines.text: מַה נַפְשָׁךְ: if the ox is yours — give it to me as payment! If it isn't yours — give it to me so I can collect from it!

lines.speaker: Q
lines.text: But didn't R' Eleazar say שְׁמִירַת נְזָקִין כִּשְׁמִירַת קִנְיָין — guarding for damages is like guarding ownership?

lines.speaker: RJ
lines.text: R' Yudan: it was handed over to him for his damages, but it remains כְּמַשְׁכּוֹן בְּיָדוֹ — like a pledge in his hand, not full ownership.

callout.label: The fault line:
callout.text: Is the goring ox transferred to the נזק as payment (R' Meir: shared ownership) or held only as collateral until value is recovered (R' Yehudah: pledge)?

slides.title: 📐 Four Test Cases — R' Yehudah vs. R' Meir
slides.subtitle: R' Yochanan: the dispute plays out in four real-life scenarios.
rows: Increase belongs to the second (the נזק)

callout.label: Why this matters:
callout.text: R' Yochanan does not abstractly resolve the dispute. He shows us what hangs on it: every benefit and burden of the ox sorts itself differently depending on whether the נזק is a partner or just a pledge-holder.

slides.title: 🤝 R' Yehudah and R' Shimon — Same Word, Different Scope
slides.subtitle: R' Yochanan's second observation: both Tannaim use 'קנה', but mean different things.

boxes.title: 🟢 R' Yehudah
items: Strong form: he is a real partner in the ox itself

boxes.title: 🔴 R' Shimon
items: Acquired only the dead (the carcass of the gored)
Weaker form: not a partner in the living goring ox

callout.label: R' Yochanan's nuance:
callout.text: שְׁנֵיהֶן אָמְרוּ דָבָר אֶחָד — they agree on the word but not its reach. Halakhic vocabulary is precise: the same verb can mean very different things in different mouths.

slides.title: 💼 Three Partners Throw Into a Purse
slides.subtitle: The sugya turns to a parallel partnership case.

nodes.content:
שלשה שהטילו לכיס
Three put different amounts into a shared purse
They gain or lose — how do they split?

columns.label: מרגלית (precious stone)
columns.content: R' Bun: split by investment — without your 10 dinarim it could not be bought

columns.label: R' Eleazar's challenge
columns.content: Even ordinary goods → split evenly: small inventory turns over fast

nodes.content: R' Eleazar's market reality: אֲנָא הֲפַךְ וּמִתְהַפֵּךְ בְּדִידִי וּמַטִּי בָךְ — I turn mine over and catch up to you

callout.label: Conceptual link:
callout.text: Just as the goring-ox sugya asks 'how does the נזק's claim translate into ownership?', this Mishnah asks 'how does each partner's investment translate into a share of the profit?' Both turn on what is being divided — capital, labor, or risk.

slides.title: 🌍 The Rome Hypothetical
slides.subtitle: R' Eleazar pushes the case across the Mediterranean.

steps.content: 1️⃣ Two partners: one with much merchandise, one with little.
2️⃣ Both have stock in the same market — small partner turns over goods quickly and catches up.
3️⃣ But what if one partner's goods are in Rome?
4️⃣ Small partner: עַד דְּאַתְּ סְלִיק לְרוֹמֵי אֲנַא הֲפַךְ וּמִתְהַפֵּךְ בְּדִידִי — by the time you travel to Rome, I've already flipped my stock.
5️⃣ Even with absent inventory, the local partner has earned an equal share through speed of trade.

callout.label: The principle:
callout.text: Capital is not the only kind of contribution. Time, motion, and the ability to keep money working in the market are themselves a form of investment that the halakhah recognizes.

summary.principle: When a tam ox damages, the Tannaim disagree whether the נזק becomes a co-owner or holds the ox as a pledge — and the same disagreement structure governs how partners share when they invest unequally.

flowSteps.content:
R' Meir attacks from two sides — yours or his?
R' Yudan: it is only a pledge

🎓 Key Principle:
Whether you call the נזק's hold on the ox ownership or pledge reshapes every consequence — and parallel partnership rules show that 'equity' depends on what you are dividing.

quiz.q: What two-sided argument does R' Yannai attribute to R' Meir?
o: Either the ox is yours and you give it as payment, or it isn't and you let me collect from it
Either you pay full damage or no damage
Either you accept the kal va-chomer or reject the Mishnah
Either the ox is mu'ad or it is tam
quiz.e: שֶׁלָּךְ הוּא תְּנֵיהוּ לוֹ. אֵינוֹ שֶׁלָּךְ תְּנֵיהוּ לִי שֶׁאַגְבֶּנּוּ — R' Meir's מה נפשך leaves the owner no way out.

quiz.q: What does R' Yudan answer in defense of R' Yehudah's view?
o: The ox truly belongs to the נזק
The ox was given over for damages but remains כְּמַשְׁכּוֹן בְּיָדוֹ — only a pledge
There is no liability at all
The ox is hekdesh
quiz.e: R' Yudan re-reads R' Eleazar: שמירת נזיקין כשמירת קניין means a pledge-like custody, not full ownership.

quiz.q: According to R' Meir, who keeps the increase if the goring ox rises in value (הוקיר)?
o: The first owner alone
The נזק (second owner) alone
Both, since they are partners
Hekdesh
quiz.e: R' Yochanan lists this as one of four test cases. R' Meir treats them as joint owners, so הוקרה לשניהם.

quiz.q: How does R' Yochanan describe R' Yehudah and R' Shimon on 'קנה'?
o: They flatly disagree
Both say קנה, but R' Yehudah means living and dead, R' Shimon means only the dead
Both say he did not acquire
Only R' Shimon says קנה
quiz.e: שְׁנֵיהֶן אָמְרוּ דָבָר אֶחָד — same word, different scope. R' Yehudah: לחי וגם למת; R' Shimon: למת בלבד.

quiz.q: In R' Eleazar's analysis of שלשה שהטילו לכיס, why might the partner with less capital still get an equal share?
o: Because the small investor turns inventory over quickly while the big investor is slow to sell
Because R' Bun says so explicitly
Because precious stones cannot be divided
Because of גזירה שווה
quiz.e: אֲנָא פְּרַגְמָטַּיָּא דִידִי קָלִיל וַאֲנַא הֲפַךְ וּמִתְהַפֵּךְ בְּדִידִי וּמַטִּי בָךְ — small inventory means fast turnover, which earns an equal share.
